Metric: Software name? (string)
URL? (URL)
Affiliation (institution(s)) (string or N/A)
Response: University of Wisconsin-Madison (primary lead; Dan Negrut, Simulation-Based Engineering Lab) and University of Parma, Italy (co-developer; Alessandro Tasora); supported by NSF, US Army, and other funders

Metric: Software purpose (string)
Response: High-performance open-source multi-physics simulation library; provides multibody dynamics, finite element analysis (FEA), rigid and flexible body simulation, vehicle dynamics (Chrono::Vehicle), granular terrain (Chrono::Granular), fluid-solid interaction (Chrono::FSI), and sensor simulation (Chrono::Sensor); supports GPU-accelerated simulation via CUDA

Metric: Number of developers (all developers that have contributed at least one commit to the project) (use repo commit logs) (number)

Last commit date? (date)

License? (GNU GPL, BSD, MIT, terms of use, trial, none, unclear, other∗) ∗ given via a string
Platforms? (set of Windows, Linux, OS X, Android, other∗) ∗ given via string

Software Category? The concept category includes software that does not have an officially released version. Public software has a released version in the public domain. Private software has a released version available to authorized users only. (concept, public, private)
Development model? (open source, freeware, commercial, unclear)
Publications about the software? Refers to publications that have used or mentioned the software. (number or unknown)
Source code URL? (set of url, n/a, unclear)
Programming language(s)? (set of FORTRAN, Matlab, C, C++, Java, R, Ruby, Python, Cython, BASIC, Pascal, IDL, unclear, other∗) ∗ given via string
Is there evidence that performance was considered? Performance refers to either speed, storage, or throughput. (yes∗, no)
Additional comments? (can cover any metrics you feel are missing, or any other thoughts you have)
Response: Project Chrono is uniquely comprehensive: rigid multibody + FEA + vehicle dynamics + GPU granular mechanics + fluid-structure interaction in one library. Chrono::Vehicle module is particularly advanced for autonomous ground vehicle simulation. PyChrono Python bindings enable scripting. Integration with Blender (Chrono::Blender) for visualization. Used by automotive industry, US Army, and NASA for off-road vehicle simulation.

Response: Core multibody simulation is very reliable. Optional GPU modules (Chrono::Granular) require correct CUDA setup for stability. Well-tested core dynamics code.

Response: Good error handling in both C++ and Python APIs. GPU out-of-memory errors for very large granular simulations are descriptive.
